does: This paper proposes Token-Aware Gradient Optimization (TAGO), a method for generating adversarial audio examples that jailbreak audio language models with sparse perturbations. By analyzing gradient distributions across audio tokens during optimization, the authors discover that gradient energy concentrates in a small subset of token-aligned regions rather than being uniformly distributed. TAGO exploits this finding by selectively updating only high-energy token regions while masking gradients elsewhere, reducing computational cost while maintaining attack effectiveness. Experiments across three audio language models show that TAGO achieves comparable attack success rates (86% on Qwen3-Omni) with only 25% of tokens retained, compared to 87% when using full waveform optimization, demonstrating that dense audio updates are redundant for jailbreak attacks.
connects: This work extends adversarial attack research on multimodal language models, building on growing interest in audio-based security threats alongside established image and text adversarial examples. It relates to gradient-based optimization attacks on neural networks and recent work on jailbreaking large language models through adversarial inputs. The paper connects to understanding token structure and interpretability in multimodal models, similar to gradient analysis in vision transformers and attention-based systems. The sparse optimization approach parallels techniques in efficient adversarial attacks and pruning methods that identify which model parameters or inputs are most critical for model behavior. The work also builds on the observation that perturbations need not be uniform, extending prior findings about minimal adversarial perturbations to the audio modality.
enables: This research opens directions for understanding which audio features are critical for model behavior and vulnerability. The sparse token identification could inform defenses by revealing which audio regions models rely on most heavily for decisions, potentially enabling targeted robustness improvements. The method suggests audio watermarking or authentication schemes that focus protection on high-gradient regions. More broadly, this work enables more efficient adversarial attack research, reducing computational barriers to studying audio model security. The token-awareness approach could transfer to other domains with token-structured inputs. Understanding sparse attack surfaces motivates future research into why models concentrate decision-making in few token regions and whether this structural weakness is fundamental to audio processing or addressable through architectural or training innovations.
